name: Utility Scoring
description: A decision model where candidate actions are scored from current context and the AI commits to the best suitable option.
design_notes: Utility scoring is useful when AI needs flexible priorities instead of rigid state transitions. It needs candidate action IDs, blackboard_key inputs, utility_score_curve definitions, score breakdown debug output, hysteresis_margin, and commitment_min_ms so behavior is explainable and stable.
design_purpose: Let AI choose between competing actions such as attack, flee, reload, cover, assist, or reposition.
Expose tunable score curves that technical designers can adjust without rewriting behavior graphs.
Create context-sensitive behavior while retaining readable debugging and commitment windows.
player_fantasy: The player feels that agents make situational choices, exploiting opportunities or protecting themselves in plausible ways.

parameters.name: candidate_action_ids
parameters.description: Actions considered during a scoring pass.

parameters.name: utility_score_curve
parameters.description: Curve or table used to convert blackboard values into action score contribution.

parameters.name: hysteresis_margin
parameters.description: Score advantage required before switching away from the current committed action.

parameters.name: commitment_min_ms
parameters.description: Minimum time the AI must keep an action before reconsidering unless interrupted by a high-priority abort.

edge_cases: All candidate actions score zero or fail validation and the AI needs a safe fallback action.
Two actions repeatedly tie and must resolve by deterministic decision order rather than random flicker.
A high-scoring action becomes invalid between scoring and commit, such as cover being reserved or target_id dying.
Networked agents need server-authoritative decisions while clients may display predicted intent.
common_bugs: Score curves are normalized inconsistently, so one blackboard_key dominates every decision.
AI switches actions every decision_interval_ms because hysteresis_margin or commitment_min_ms is missing.
Debug output shows final score but not contribution breakdown, making tuning impossible.
Candidate actions run side effects during scoring instead of only during committed execution.
balancing_notes: Score curves should be tuned from observed encounters; theoretical priority values often fail once actions combine.
Commitment windows make AI readable but too much commitment can make agents ignore urgent threats.
Use per-archetype score weights so all enemies do not converge on the same optimal behavior.
accessibility_notes: Readable intent cues such as windups, barks, or posture changes help players understand why AI suddenly changes tactics.
Avoid perfect reaction scoring for required counterplay; give players timing windows after AI commits to an action.

implementation_notes.unity: Define candidate actions and utility_score_curve assets separately from MonoBehaviours; each decision pass should output a debug_reason_trace with blackboard_key inputs and score contributions.
implementation_notes.godot: Store action definitions as Resources, evaluate blackboard dictionaries on a timer, and keep scoring pure so only the selected action mutates AI state.
implementation_notes.unreal: Use Utility AI alongside Behavior Trees/StateTree by writing selected action IDs to Blackboard; Visual Logger should record score breakdowns, aborts, and commitment timers.
implementation_notes.web: Run scoring in deterministic candidate_action_ids order, keep curves as data, and log score traces for replayable bug reports and AI-agent debugging.

example_games.title: The Sims
example_games.note: Uses needs and context to drive agent choices.

example_games.title: XCOM
example_games.note: Enemy decisions can be understood as evaluating tactical options such as cover, attack, and movement.
